title: German (Germany) Style Guide

children.text: Target audience: Developers building with Sanity, content editors managing structured content, and technical leaders evaluating content infrastructure in Germany, Austria, and Switzerland.
Gender guidance: Use gender-inclusive language. Prefer the gender colon form (“Entwickler:innen”, “Redakteur:innen”) for inclusive references to people.
Address the reader with “Sie” (formal). Avoid unnecessary anglicisms when established German terms exist, but keep Sanity product names in English.
“Inhalte im Content Lake speichern” (keep product name in English)
“Inhalte im Inhaltssee speichern” (do not translate product names)

title: French (France) Style Guide

children.text: Target audience: Developers integrating Sanity into front-end applications, and content teams managing editorial workflows in France.

Follow Académie française recommendations for French equivalents of English tech terms where established alternatives exist. Keep Sanity product names (Studio, Content Lake, GROQ, Portable Text) in English.
“Interrogez vos contenus avec GROQ” (keep product name, use vous)
“le jeu de données de production” (use French term for dataset)
“action de document” for document action, “champ” for field
“le Content Lake de Sanity” without first introducing the term in context

title: Japanese (Japan) Style Guide

children.text: Target audience: Developers evaluating Sanity for content infrastructure, and content editors who will use Studio daily in Japan.

Use desu/masu (です/ます) form throughout. Keep Sanity product names in their original Latin script. Write technical terms in katakana when no established Japanese equivalent exists. Use full-width punctuation (。、) consistently.
“Sanity Studioを設定します” (product name in Latin script, desu/masu form)
“コンテンツをContent Lakeに保存します” (product name in Latin script)
“データセット” for dataset, “フィールド” for field
“サニティ” (do not transliterate “Sanity” to katakana)
“コンテントレイク” (do not transliterate “Content Lake”)

title: How to Run a Simultaneous Product Launch Across 12 Markets Without Losing Your Mind
excerpt: Coordinating a same‑day product launch across a dozen markets is less about heroics and more about ruthless structure. This article walks through the real-world mechanics: timezone choreography, translation and localization pipelines, regulatory review, asset production, and how structured content makes a 24‑hour global go-live actually work.

children.text: Why a 24-Hour Global Launch Is a Different Beast

Launching in 12 markets within the same 24-hour window is not just "more work" than a single-market launch; it’s a different category of problem. You’re no longer shipping a campaign; you’re orchestrating a distributed operation with legal, linguistic, and technical constraints that don’t care about your master deck. The teams that pull this off consistently don’t rely on heroics in the final week. They rely on structure: content models, decision logs, localization pipelines, and a brutally clear run-of-show. Think of it less as a marketing project and more as a release train that happens to include ads, landing pages, and PR.

The goal is simple: every market goes live with the right message, in the right language, on the right channels, in the same 24-hour window. The reality is messy: a regulator in Germany wants a new disclaimer, your Japanese partner flags your product name as awkward, and Brazil’s app store screenshots are still stuck in review. The way through is to design your launch system so that these issues are expected, absorbed, and resolved without derailing the global timeline.
Start With a Timezone-First Launch Architecture

Most teams plan launches in their home timezone and then "add markets." That’s how you end up asking your Tokyo team to run a 3 a.m. go-live. Instead, design the launch around a 24-hour clock that starts where your earliest market wakes up and ends where your latest market signs off.
(e.g., 00:00–23:59 UTC) and then map each market’s local go-live time into that window. For example, you might decide:
APAC markets go live between 02:00–06:00 UTC

: APAC is the first to go live and the first to report issues; EMEA is on deck to adjust based on APAC learnings; Americas benefits from both. This isn’t about changing the campaign; it’s about tightening copy, fixing broken links, or adjusting FAQs before the next wave. You need a staffed
—not one exhausted global PM on Slack. Assign explicit roles by region and shift: incident commander, content approver, engineering liaison, and analytics lead.
Structured Content: The Only Way This Scales
The difference between a smooth global launch and a chaotic one is usually the content model. If your assets live as static files in random folders—
: discrete, reusable fields that can be localized, approved, and deployed independently.
At minimum, model your content like this:
: name, short description, long description, feature bullets, legal disclaimers, pricing notes, and internal IDs.
: web hero, app store listing, email subject lines, paid social headlines, PR boilerplate—each as separate fields, not embedded in a PDF.
: source language, required locales, character limits, tone guidelines, and regulatory tags (e.g.,
When everything is structured, you can push a last-minute legal change (e.g., a new disclaimer for France) to the single
disclaimer field and have it propagate to web, app store, and email templates automatically. Without this, you’re manually editing 40 files at 2 a.m. and hoping you didn’t miss one.
Building a Translation and Localization Pipeline That Won’t Break
A simultaneous launch across 12 markets is where ad-hoc translation dies. You can’t email a deck to an agency and hope for the best. You need a
with clear stages, SLAs, and ownership.
: Lock English (or your source language) copy for v1 at least 4–6 weeks before launch. Changes after this point go through a change-control process.
: Use a translation management system (TMS) integrated with your CMS. Translators work directly on structured fields, with character limits and context (screenshots, UX flows) visible.
: Local marketing or product owners review for nuance, brand fit, and cultural issues. This is where you catch that your clever pun doesn’t work in Italian.
: Review strings in context in staging builds. You’re checking truncation, line breaks, and whether the UI still makes sense.

For example, we once shipped a “Boost” feature where the English tagline was “Tap to Boost your reach.” In German, the literal translation overflowed buttons and looked clumsy. Because we had structured strings and a TMS integration, the German team could propose a shorter variant just for button labels (
) while keeping the longer phrase in body copy. No code changes, no emergency design work—just updating the right field.
Naming Disasters and How to Avoid Them

Product names are where global launches most often go sideways. A name that’s safe in English can be awkward, offensive, or simply ridiculous elsewhere. I’ve seen a perfectly innocent English name that, in Brazilian Portuguese slang, was uncomfortably close to a sexual term; another that in Mandarin sounded like a cheap knock-off of a competitor.
The fix is not “ask the translator at the end.” You need a

: Before you fall in love with a name, run it through in-market stakeholders or a naming agency across your target languages. You’re looking for phonetic clashes, negative connotations, and trademark conflicts.
Define a naming strategy per market

: Some markets can use the English name as-is; others need transliteration; some need a fully localized name. For example, you might keep “Pulse” in Western Europe but use a localized name in Japan that conveys “insight” rather than “heartbeat,” if the latter feels too medical.
Map aliases in your content model
. That way, your Japanese app store listing can use the localized name, while your legal docs and internal systems still reference the global name.
The key is to treat naming as part of the product system, not a last-minute branding flourish. Once a bad name ships globally, you’ll spend months cleaning up confusion and SEO fallout.
Regulatory Review: Design for the Slowest Regulator

Regulatory review is where global timelines quietly die. Different jurisdictions have different rules on claims, disclosures, data handling, and even which screenshots you can show. If you design your process around the fastest regulator, you’ll slip. Instead, design around the
Start by tagging every claim and asset with
, etc. Then map which markets require which reviews. For example:
The US might require legal review for any performance claim.
Germany might require specific data privacy language.
Japan might restrict certain before/after visuals.

: rows are markets, columns are asset types and claim types, cells define who must sign off and by when. Then schedule reviews backward from launch, with buffer. If Germany needs 10 business days for legal review, your German copy freeze is 2 weeks earlier than the US.

One hard lesson: never assume that a global “master disclaimer” is acceptable everywhere. In one launch, a generic “results may vary” line was fine in the US but considered misleading in the UK, which required more specific language. Because disclaimers were structured fields per locale, we could update the UK copy without touching other markets or rebuilding assets.
Asset Localization: Screenshots, Videos, and UI Strings
Static copy is the easy part. The real complexity shows up in
App store screenshots per platform and locale
Landing page hero images with embedded text
In-product tooltips, empty states, and onboarding flows
The trap is baking text into images and videos. Every time you do that, you create a new localization track. Instead:
: Use UI captures and icons, keep text in overlays or captions that can be swapped per locale.
: Maintain master Figma/PSD/AE files where text layers are clearly labeled and mapped to content fields. Your localization team should never be editing flattened PNGs.
: Define a set of canonical screens (e.g., home, detail, settings) and capture them in each language from a localized build. Name them systematically (
) and link them in your CMS.

For example, when we launched a new analytics dashboard, we created a single motion graphics template where all on-screen text pulled from a JSON file keyed by locale. To update the Japanese version, we didn’t re-edit the video; we swapped in
and re-rendered. That’s the leverage structured content gives you.
that reads more like an operations playbook than a marketing plan. It should be timeboxed in UTC and broken down by hour, with clear owners and decision thresholds.
: final content freezes, staging environment sign-off, dry runs of publishing workflows, and rollback procedures.
: pre-flight checks per market (feature flags set, payment methods tested, tracking pixels firing, app store approvals confirmed).
: exact go-live times per channel (web, app, email, paid), who presses which button, and what constitutes a “stop the train” issue.
: APAC logs issues in a single global incident channel with a standard template (impact, scope, workaround). EMEA reviews and decides whether to adjust upcoming launches or proceed as planned.

. A broken purchase flow is a non-negotiable; you pause the next region. A minor translation nit in a tooltip is a flexible; you log it and fix in the next patch. Decide this before launch day, not in the heat of the moment.
Data, Feedback Loops, and Post-Launch Hygiene

The advantage of a staggered 24-hour window is that you can learn from early markets. The risk is overreacting to noisy early data. Set expectations: APAC performance in the first 4 hours is directional, not definitive. Use it to catch
, not to rewrite your value proposition.
Instrument your launch so that you can see, per market and per channel:

for the first 72 hours: prioritized issues that can be fixed without destabilizing the system (FAQ updates, minor copy tweaks, clarifying UI labels). Because your content is structured, these changes should be low-risk and fast to deploy.

within 2–3 weeks with representatives from all regions. Document what broke, what saved you, and what needs to change in your content model, workflows, or regulatory matrix. The goal is not a perfect launch; it’s a launch system that gets more resilient every time you use it.
The Real Lesson: Treat Global Launch as Infrastructure
After you’ve done a few of these, you stop thinking of a global launch as a one-off event and start treating it as
. The content model, localization pipeline, regulatory matrix, and run-of-show are assets you reuse and refine, not documents you reinvent.
The teams that consistently hit same-day global launches aren’t the ones with the flashiest creative; they’re the ones with:
Structured content that separates meaning from presentation
Clear ownership across timezones and functions
Localization built into the process, not bolted on at the end
Regulatory constraints modeled into their workflows

If you invest in that backbone early, a 12-market, 24-hour launch stops being a heroic feat and becomes just another train on the schedule—complex, yes, but predictable, repeatable, and ultimately a competitive advantage.

title: Why Translation Glossaries Are Your Most Underrated Content Asset
excerpt: Most teams treat translation glossaries as dusty spreadsheets. In reality, a well-structured glossary—modeled as metadata instead of static tables—is infrastructure that drives quality, consistency, and speed across every market. Here’s how to design glossaries that actually guide human and AI translators to make the right decisions, every time.

children.text: Most companies have a translation glossary somewhere. It usually lives in a spreadsheet, was last updated two rebrands ago, and no one is quite sure which column is the source of truth. Yet that same company is surprised when product copy, help center articles, and campaigns come back from localization with inconsistent terminology, awkward grammar, and brand names mysteriously translated in some markets but not others. The problem isn’t just the translators. The problem is that the glossary is treated as documentation, not as infrastructure.

When you treat a glossary as infrastructure, you stop thinking of it as a static table and start thinking of it as structured metadata that every translation agent—human or AI—can query and enforce. Instead of a row that says
Widget Pro | Widgit Pro | Product name

, you have a structured entry: term, status, part of speech, grammatical gender (where relevant), usage context, and rules like “never translate” or “flag if used.” That structure is what lets tools and workflows make smart decisions automatically, instead of relying on someone scanning a spreadsheet at 11 p.m. before a release.

Take the simplest case: a product name that must never be translated. In a spreadsheet, you might have a note in a comments column: “Brand name, keep in English.” In practice, that note is easy to miss and impossible for an AI system to reliably interpret. As structured metadata, the entry might look like this:
"Product name for our multi-tenant SaaS platform; appears in UI, marketing, and legal docs"
Now your translation memory system, CAT tool, or LLM-based agent can do something useful: automatically lock the term, prevent target-language edits, and flag any attempt to localize it into
Centro en la Nube de Acme
in Spanish. Human translators see a clear, machine-enforced rule instead of a vague comment. AI agents can be prompted with explicit instructions derived from the metadata: “If
The value becomes even clearer with technical terms that
be translated—but only in one specific way. Imagine you’re localizing a cloud security product into German. You have the English term
, which in your domain is a specific AWS-style construct, not a generic group concerned with security. A robust glossary entry might be:
"AWS-style network access control entity; use with definite article 'die' and plural 'Sicherheitsgruppen'"
With that metadata, a German translator (or AI) doesn’t just know
to use it in a sentence. They won’t write

. Your style checker can validate that the correct gender and plural form are used in UI strings. An LLM can be instructed: “Use the target term exactly as specified; respect gender and plural guidance; do not create synonyms.” That’s a level of control you simply don’t get from a free-text note in a spreadsheet.

Structured glossaries are also how you retire bad language from your ecosystem. Most organizations have deprecated terms that still haunt their content: old product names, outdated industry jargon, or phrases that no longer align with brand or legal requirements. In a static glossary, you might bold them or add a comment like “don’t use anymore.” In a metadata-driven glossary, you give them a first-class status:
"Legacy term in billing; replace in all new and updated content; still appears in some screenshots and logs"
Now your pipeline can do something powerful. When a translator or AI agent encounters
, the system can automatically flag it, suggest

, and require an explicit override if the old term must be preserved (for example, in a legal citation or UI that hasn’t yet been updated). Over time, this turns your glossary into a governance mechanism, not just a reference document. You’re not just telling people what words you like; you’re actively shaping the language of your product and brand.

The real unlock is when this structured glossary becomes a shared service across your stack. Your design system, CMS, help center platform, and translation tools all query the same glossary API. When a product manager names a new feature, they check for collisions and forbidden patterns before it ever hits a roadmap. When marketing drafts a campaign, their writing environment auto-suggests approved terms and warns on deprecated ones. When you plug in an LLM to generate localized help content, you don’t just give it a PDF style guide—you give it a machine-readable glossary and instruct it to treat those entries as hard constraints.

This is why glossaries are infrastructure, not documentation. Documentation is something people might read if they remember and have time. Infrastructure is something systems depend on. When your glossary is modeled as structured metadata—term, status, part of speech, gender, usage context, translatability, preferred alternatives—it stops being a dusty spreadsheet and becomes a control plane for language. It lets you scale into new markets without sacrificing precision, keeps your AI agents on-brand and on-terminology, and turns every translation from a best-effort guess into an informed, governed decision. In a world where content is increasingly generated and transformed by machines, that control plane is not a nice-to-have; it’s the backbone of your multilingual strategy.

title: Designing Locale-Specific Style Guides That Preserve Brand Identity
excerpt: How to build per-locale style guides that keep your brand recognizable while adapting formality, tone, and messaging to Germany, Japan, Brazil, and beyond.

children.text: Most global brands underestimate how much

need to shift across markets. They’ll translate copy, maybe tweak a headline, but keep the same “voice” brief in English for every locale. Then they wonder why the German site feels cold, the Japanese site feels off-brand, and the Brazilian social channels feel like a different company. The missing piece is a
that treats language and culture as first-class design constraints, not afterthoughts.
A good locale-specific style guide doesn’t reinvent your brand; it

within the norms of that culture. That means being explicit about formality level, tone, and audience expectations in each market. “Professional,” “friendly,” or “casual” are not universal settings you can toggle on and off—they manifest very differently in Germany, Japan, and Brazil, even when the underlying brand promise is identical.
Take “professional” as a starting point. In
. A German B2B SaaS landing page might say:
Mit unserer Plattform optimieren Sie Ihre Lieferkette und reduzieren Kosten um bis zu 20 %.
form, the direct claim, and the emphasis on measurable outcomes. Jokes are rare, idioms are used sparingly, and bulletproof terminology matters. A German style guide should spell this out:
for all customer-facing content unless explicitly targeting a youth or startup audience.
Readers expect well-structured arguments, clear benefits, and correct technical vocabulary; avoid hype and vague promises.
If your global brand voice is “friendly and casual,” the German version might become
—friendly, but grounded and serious. That’s still on-brand, just expressed in a culturally credible way.
, “professional” is less about technical density and more about
. The default for most written customer communication is the
and careful avoidance of anything that could feel pushy or confrontational. A Japanese onboarding email that mirrors an English “Hey, welcome aboard!” might instead read:
この度は弊社サービスにご登録いただき、誠にありがとうございます。快適にご利用いただけるよう、簡単なご案内をお送りいたします。

Literally: “Thank you very much for registering for our service. We will send you a brief guide so that you can use it comfortably.” It’s polite, slightly indirect, and focused on the customer’s comfort. A Japanese style guide should specify:
form; avoid plain form in all official communications.
ていねい、しんせつ、しんらいできる、ひかえめ
Readers value humility and clarity; avoid aggressive calls to action and over-claiming. Prefer softeners like
If your English brand is “friendly and casual,” your Japanese articulation might be
—still approachable, but with a clear hierarchy and politeness baked into every sentence ending.
. Brazilian Portuguese customer comms frequently use
, inclusive language, and a conversational tone—even in B2B contexts. A Brazilian push notification that in English reads, “Your report is ready” might become:
Seu relatório está pronto! Dá uma olhada nos insights que separamos pra você.
. A Brazilian style guide should clarify:
only for very specific, older, or highly formal audiences.
Readers are comfortable with a bit of slang in the right channels (social, email), but expect clarity and respect when discussing money, contracts, or support issues.
For a “friendly and casual” global brand, Brazil can often stay
—but your style guide should still define boundaries: which slang is acceptable, which emojis are okay in which channels, and when to dial up formality (e.g., legal notices, outages, or apologies).

. If you enforce a single, rigid voice globally, you’ll be consistent—but you’ll sound tone-deaf or even rude in some markets. If you let every locale do whatever feels natural, you’ll be culturally aligned—but your brand will fragment. The way out is to distinguish between

Non-negotiables are things like: what your brand stands for, your core personality traits (e.g., “helpful,” “transparent,” “optimistic”), and your stance on honesty and inclusivity. Adaptables are things like: level of formality, directness vs. indirectness, humor style, and how much emotional language you use. Your per-locale style guides should explicitly map how the same non-negotiables show up differently:
We are friendly, transparent, and on the customer’s side.
(obliging) but structured; Transparent → detailed, precise explanations; On your side → strong support commitments, clear SLAs.
Japan: Friendly → warm but modest; Transparent → clear, step-by-step guidance without overwhelming; On your side → attentive support, proactive apologies, and reassurance.
Brazil: Friendly → warm, conversational; Transparent → plain-language explanations, no jargon; On your side → enthusiastic encouragement and quick, informal support.
is a working document, not a manifesto. For each language/market, I recommend at least the following sections:
Explicitly state pronoun choices (Sie vs. du, desu/masu vs. plain, você vs. o senhor/a senhora).
Define formality by channel: website, product UI, email, support chat, social, legal.
Include 3–5 example sentences rewritten at the correct formality level.
Choose 4–6 adjectives that translate your global voice into local terms (e.g., for Japan:
Who are we talking to, realistically, in this market? Age range, job roles, digital literacy, expectations of foreign brands.

What they worry about, what they value, and what they find off-putting (e.g., German engineers hate vague claims; Japanese SMB owners dislike aggressive upsell; Brazilian consumers dislike overly cold, robotic messaging).
How to handle apologies, bad news, and outages.
Rules for humor, emojis, and cultural references.
Specific taboo topics or phrases to avoid.
Localization notes for dates, numbers, honorifics, and name order.
This doesn’t need to be a 60-page PDF; a tight 6–10 page doc per locale, with lots of examples, is usually enough to align writers, translators, and agencies.
To make this concrete, imagine a global tagline in English:
“We’ve got your back, every step of the way.”
Here’s how a style guide might steer adaptations:
Style guide note: Emphasize reliability and support; avoid overly colloquial metaphors.
Wir begleiten Sie zuverlässig auf jedem Schritt Ihres Weges.
Rationale: Keeps the journey metaphor but adds
Style guide note: Focus on安心 (peace of mind); use desu/masu; soften absolute claims.
お客さまの歩みに、いつも寄り添うパートナーでありたいと考えています。
Rationale: “We wish to be a partner who is always by your side in your journey” — humble, aspirational, polite.
Style guide note: Sound close and encouraging;
A gente tá com você em cada passo dessa jornada.
All three lines are recognizably the same brand promise, but each is tuned to local expectations. This is the job of a per-locale style guide: to
codify these choices so they’re repeatable
, not one-off magic from a single good translator.
The teams that do this well treat style guides as

. They review them every 6–12 months with local marketers, writers, and support leads. They collect real examples—emails that performed well, support replies that delighted customers, social posts that backfired—and fold those learnings back into the document. They also make the tension between global and local explicit: when in doubt, which side wins? For some brands, local cultural fit is paramount; for others, strict global consistency is non-negotiable.
If you’re starting from scratch, begin with your

and build short, opinionated style guides that cover formality, tone adjectives, audience description, and additional instructions with concrete examples. Use them in real workflows (briefing agencies, training translators, onboarding new writers), then refine. Over time, you’ll end up with a set of locale-specific guides that let your brand feel
, while sounding like it actually belongs in each place it speaks.

title: Structured Metadata Makes AI Translation Actually Useful
excerpt: Raw AI translation is impressive, but it’s not production-ready on its own. The missing ingredient is structured metadata: glossaries, style guides, and content-aware filtering that turn a generic model into a dependable translation engine for your brand.

children.text: Most teams discover the same thing the first time they try to “just use ChatGPT” for translation: the demo looks magical, the first batch of content is

, and then the edge cases start to bite. Product names get translated when they shouldn’t. Legal copy drifts into friendly marketing tone. A single English term ends up with three different translations across your app, docs, and emails. The model is powerful—but raw AI translation is not the same as production-grade localization.

The missing layer isn’t more model magic; it’s structure. Specifically, structured metadata about your content and your brand: glossaries, style guides, and locale rules that are systematically injected into every translation prompt. Without that structure, you’re effectively pasting text into a very smart black box and hoping for the best. With it, you’re running a repeatable translation pipeline that behaves like a seasoned localization team.

Let’s start with glossaries. In theory, you can tell an AI model, “Don’t translate product names” or “Use this term for ‘workspace’ in German.” In practice, unless those rules are captured as structured metadata and programmatically attached to each translation job, they will be forgotten, misapplied, or overridden by a well-meaning human operator in a hurry. A glossary is not a Google Sheet someone might remember to paste into the prompt; it’s a maintained, versioned list of terms—source, target, part of speech, notes, and do-not-translate flags—that the system can reliably enforce.

I’ve seen what happens without this. A B2B SaaS platform had a feature called “Flowboard.” In English, it’s a coined brand term. In Spanish, the raw AI translation happily rendered it as “Tablero de flujo” in some places, left it as “Flowboard” in others, and once even turned it into “Panel de flujo de trabajo” because the surrounding context looked like workflow management. All of these are linguistically defensible; none of them are acceptable from a brand perspective. A structured glossary with
would have prevented the model from getting creative in the first place.

Glossaries also matter for non-branded terminology. If your product team has decided that “workspace” is always “Arbeitsbereich” in German (not “Arbeitsraum” or “Workspace” in English), that decision needs to be encoded, not remembered. The same goes for domain-specific language: medical, financial, legal, or gaming terms that have one

translation and several plausible but wrong ones. Raw AI translation will often pick something that sounds fine to a general speaker but is unacceptable to a specialist. A structured glossary gives the model a map of your domain so it doesn’t have to guess.
The second big gap between raw AI and production-grade translation is style. Models are very good at sounding fluent; they are much less consistent at sounding like

. A French marketing email should not read like a French legal disclaimer. A Japanese in-app tooltip should not sound like a customer support ticket. Yet when you paste text into ChatGPT with a vague instruction like “translate to French,” you’re leaving register, tone, and formality entirely up to the model’s defaults and training data.
This is where structured style guides come in. A real style guide is more than “be friendly.” It encodes decisions like:
German: use "du" for consumer app UI, "Sie" for invoices and legal.
Japanese: prefer neutral, concise phrasing for UI; avoid overly polite keigo in tooltips.
Spanish: keep English product names in Roman script; don’t localize trademark symbols; use sentence case for button labels.
French marketing: confident and clear, avoid anglicisms unless they are industry standard.

When this guidance is stored as structured metadata per locale and content type, your translation agent can assemble the right style instructions automatically: “You are translating in-app UI strings for a consumer mobile app, German locale, using informal
voice, short labels, and preserving English product names.” That’s a very different outcome from “Translate this into German.”

The third piece—content-aware filtering—is the one most teams underestimate, and it’s where the naive “paste text into ChatGPT” approach really falls apart at scale. If you dump your entire global glossary into every prompt, you overwhelm the model with irrelevant instructions. You also increase the chance that it will try to force a glossary term into a context where it doesn’t belong, simply because it was told those terms are important.

I’ve seen prompts that start with three pages of glossary entries, followed by a couple of paragraphs of actual content. The result is predictable: the model either ignores half the glossary because it can’t keep it all in working memory, or it over-applies terms in awkward ways. For example, a support article about “accounts” starts using the specialized “Account” term from your billing domain glossary, even though in this context “account” just means “user profile.” The intent was good; the targeting was missing.

Content-aware filtering fixes this by treating glossaries as data, not as static prompt text. Instead of shoving everything into the model, your pipeline selects only the terms that are actually relevant to the content being translated. That can be as simple as matching source strings against glossary entries, or as sophisticated as using embeddings to find semantically related terms. The key is that for a given translation job, the agent sees a
glossary: the 10–30 terms that matter for this piece of content, not the 2,000 terms your company has ever defined.
Imagine translating a release note about a new “Workspace Sharing” feature. A content-aware pipeline might:
Detect that the content is about the
Pull only the glossary entries tagged with those domains (e.g.,
even though they exist in the master glossary.
The resulting prompt is focused: the model knows exactly which terms are sacred and which it can translate freely.

Now, contrast this with the common “just paste it into ChatGPT” workflow. A marketer copies a page of English copy, opens a new chat, types: “Translate this into French, keeping the brand tone,” and hits send. Maybe they paste in a few key terms. Maybe they don’t. The output looks fine, they ship it, and everyone feels productive. Until three months later, when you try to unify your French site and discover that the same CTA is translated three different ways, your product name has been localized in some places and not others, and your legal team is nervous about the tone of your French terms of service.

The problem isn’t that the model is bad; it’s that the process is ad hoc. Every translation is a one-off experiment with slightly different instructions, different context, and different human judgment. There’s no guarantee that the same input tomorrow will produce the same output, because the prompt, the glossary, and the style guidance are all living in someone’s head or in a random document.
A structured pipeline looks very different. Instead of humans crafting prompts by hand, you have a translation agent that assembles prompts from metadata at runtime. The inputs aren’t just
Identifies the content type and locale.
Is this UI copy, marketing, help center, or legal? Which language and region?
Pull the locale-specific rules for this content type: formality, tone, formatting, examples.
Select only the terms relevant to this content, based on tags, matches, or semantic similarity.
Things like date formats, number formats, currency handling, and whether to translate certain categories of terms.
A consistent template that includes instructions, examples, glossary, and the source text in a predictable format.

From the model’s perspective, every translation job arrives with a clear contract: “Here’s who you are, what you’re doing, which terms are non-negotiable, and how the output should look.” That’s how you get repeatability.

The payoff is not just better language quality; it’s operational sanity. When your glossaries and style guides are structured, you can version them, audit them, and roll out changes safely. If you decide that

should now be translated differently in German, you update the glossary once and re-run affected content. If your French team wants to shift from formal to semi-formal tone in marketing emails, you update the style guide metadata and every future translation reflects that decision—no retraining, no mass re-education of prompt writers.

You also get traceability. When someone asks, “Why is this term translated this way?” you can point to the glossary entry and the style guide rule that drove the output. With the paste-into-ChatGPT approach, the honest answer is usually, “Because that’s what the model felt like doing that day, based on a prompt we didn’t save.” That’s not a satisfying answer when you’re dealing with regulated industries, high-stakes messaging, or just a brand that takes itself seriously.

I’m opinionated about this because I’ve seen both sides. Raw AI translation is fantastic for exploration, quick drafts, and one-off tasks. It’s the new baseline. But if you care about consistency, brand integrity, and the ability to scale across dozens of locales and content types, you can’t treat translation as a chat session. You need to treat it as a system.

That system is built on structured metadata: glossaries that lock in approved terminology and protect your brand names, style guides that enforce the right register and tone per locale, and content-aware filtering that keeps prompts lean and relevant. The model is important, but it’s not the differentiator anymore. The differentiator is how well you wrap that model in structure.

If your current process still involves copying and pasting into a chat window, consider that your proof-of-concept phase. It showed you what’s possible. The next step—the one that actually makes AI translation useful in production—is turning your tribal knowledge into structured metadata and letting an agent do the boring, critical work of assembling the right prompt every single time.

title: The Content Reuse Playbook for Multi‑Market Teams
excerpt: How to structure, model, and govern content so global teams can reuse, adapt, and localize efficiently—without breaking the source.

children.text: If you run content across more than three markets, you eventually hit the same wall: every region wants flexibility, every HQ wants consistency, and everyone is drowning in duplicated docs, rogue translations, and half‑updated campaigns. The way out isn’t more guidelines or more meetings; it’s structuring content so it can be reused intentionally. That means deciding what gets translated, what gets transcreated, what must be created locally—and backing those decisions with a content model and workflow that make the right behavior the easiest behavior.
Let’s start with the simplest layer: content that should be

. This is anything where precision and consistency matter more than creative nuance. Think product descriptions, feature lists, pricing tables, API docs, help center articles, and legal copy. If your German product page describes a feature differently from your English one, you don’t have localization, you have a product risk. In practice, this means treating this content as

in your CMS: one canonical product description per SKU, one canonical help article per topic, with localized variants linked to that source—not copied into separate pages. Translation memory and terminology management plug into this layer very well.
Next is content that should be

—rebuilt for each market while preserving the strategic intent. This is your brand storytelling, hero campaigns, taglines, social concepts, and high‑visibility landing pages. A tagline that works in the US might be tone‑deaf in Japan, even if translated perfectly. Here, the source content is not a master to be obeyed word‑for‑word; it’s a
. For example, instead of a single blob of copy for a campaign hero, your model might separate:
. Local teams then transcreate within those constraints, keeping the same promise and proof, but changing the story, references, and idioms.

. This is anything that depends on local context: market‑specific case studies, references to local partners, content about local regulations, or thought leadership tied to regional trends. A fintech in Brazil will need content about PIX and local compliance; a German B2B SaaS might need deep dives into BaFin expectations. Trying to centralize this content leads to slow, generic output that nobody trusts. Instead, your global model should define
—and give markets ownership of those slots while still enforcing structure and metadata.
Structured content models are what make this whole system workable. The core principle is simple:
. For example, instead of hard‑coding a product description into every landing page, you create a
. Your landing pages then reference the
object. When the long description changes, every page updates automatically. Localized versions are stored as variants of the same
entity, not as independent pages that can drift.
A concrete scenario: imagine a global campaign for a new analytics feature. In a structured model, you’d have a

and overrides specific fields: maybe the UK team changes the headline and social copy (transcreation), but reuses the global explainer video and product screenshots (translation or straight reuse). The CMS enforces that every localization must still reference the same
ID, so reporting and governance stay intact.
The organizational piece is where most teams fail. You need

that reflect these content types. For translated content, the workflow is typically: global author → legal/compliance (if needed) → localization manager → in‑market reviewer. The source object is locked for structural changes once approved; local teams can propose edits, but those flow back into the global source, not into ad‑hoc local variants. For transcreated content, the workflow is more collaborative: global defines the strategy and guardrails, local teams own the final wording and creative, but must map each asset back to the global brief fields. Your CMS should support role‑based permissions so local teams can edit
but not touch global system fields like
One pattern that works well is the

model. Guardrails are the structured, non‑negotiable elements: product facts, claims, mandatory legal lines, and brand constraints. These live in shared content types and are reused everywhere via references. The playground is where local teams can adapt: optional content blocks, local examples, testimonials, and channel‑specific copy. In your model, this might look like a
array that markets can populate with pre‑approved block types (e.g.,
). This keeps the skeleton consistent while letting the flesh change by market.
To make this sustainable, you also need

. Define which content types are global‑owned, which are locally owned, and which are shared. Document this in a living content playbook, but more importantly, encode it into your tools: workflows, permissions, required fields, and content templates. Track reuse: how many markets are referencing the same product description object? How many are using the global campaign assets vs. uploading their own? When you see a market constantly bypassing the model—e.g., creating standalone pages instead of using the shared types—that’s a signal your structure doesn’t match their reality. Fix the model, not just the behavior.
The teams that win at multi‑market content don’t produce more; they
produce once, structure well, and reuse intelligently

. Translation keeps your facts aligned, transcreation keeps your brand resonant, and local creation keeps you relevant. The glue is a content model that encodes these choices and workflows that let local teams adapt without breaking the source. If you get those three layers working together, you stop arguing about who owns what and start focusing on what actually matters: whether the right message is reaching the right customer, in the right market, at the right moment.

title: How to Run Seasonal Campaigns Across Cultures Without Losing Your Mind (or Your ROI)
excerpt: Seasonal and holiday campaigns don’t travel well by default. “Back to School” means August in the US but April in Japan, Black Friday matters wildly different amounts by market, and local holidays like Singles Day, Diwali, and Golden Week can dwarf Western tentpoles. Here’s a practical, battle-tested approach to planning and operating a global seasonal content calendar.

children.text: Running seasonal campaigns across markets is where otherwise sophisticated teams quietly lose a lot of money. The problem isn’t just translation; it’s that seasons, school years, and shopping rituals are culturally defined. If you treat your US calendar as the “master” and simply localize, you’ll miss key demand spikes and push irrelevant messages at the wrong time. I’ve seen global brands run a big “Back to School” push in August in Japan, only to discover that Japanese parents did their big school shopping in March and April, aligned to the April school year start. By the time the global campaign hit, the budget was gone and the shelves were already stocked.

Take “Back to School” as a simple example. In the US, it’s a late July–August story: backpacks, laptops, dorm kits. In Japan, the school year starts in April, with major purchasing in March; in Australia, it’s January–February. If you’re a global laptop brand and you anchor your media and content production to the US calendar, you’ll be late in Japan and early in Australia. The fix is not three different campaigns; it’s one modular campaign with market-specific timing and emphasis. The core creative platform (e.g., “New Year, New Skills”) stays constant, but the visual cues (cherry blossoms and uniforms in Japan, summer break ending in the US) and the promotional windows shift.

Black Friday is another trap. In the US, it’s a cultural event: people understand doorbusters, limited-time chaos, and the Friday–Monday arc into Cyber Monday. In Western Europe, Black Friday exists but is still a bit of an imported concept; in some markets it’s mostly an excuse for a generic November sale. In others, it’s nearly irrelevant or even viewed skeptically. I’ve worked with teams in Germany where heavy Black Friday messaging felt off-brand and price-obsessed, while in Brazil the same brand saw huge lifts because consumers had already embraced the event as a major discount moment. The lesson: don’t assume Black Friday is your global tentpole. In some markets, it’s a tactical promo; in others, it’s background noise compared to local events.

Then you have market-specific holidays that are bigger than any Western import. Singles Day (11.11) in China is the obvious one: it’s not just a sale, it’s an e‑commerce supernova with its own rituals, expectations, and platform-specific playbooks. Diwali in India is another: it’s about light, family, and renewal, with strong gifting and home-upgrade behavior. Golden Week in Japan is a cluster of holidays that drives travel, leisure, and big-ticket purchases. If your global calendar doesn’t explicitly prioritize these, you’re effectively telling local teams that their biggest revenue moments are secondary to global brand beats. That’s how you end up with a beautifully localized Halloween campaign in India that nobody asked for, while Diwali gets a last-minute banner swap.

The real operational challenge is not knowing these dates; it’s being ready for them. Seasonal content has a long tail of dependencies: photography that reflects local weather and dress, copy that matches cultural tone (e.g., Diwali’s optimism vs. Singles Day’s playful self-indulgence), legal reviews for local regulations, and merchandising alignment. If you want to be live in early April for Japan’s Back to School, you’re locking creative in January and shooting assets in December. That means your global content planning needs a 6–9 month horizon, with a clear view of which markets need which assets when. Teams that don’t do this end up reusing US imagery of kids in hoodies and fall leaves for a Japanese spring campaign—and local teams quietly hate it.

Localization is not just text. Visual design and tone are where most global campaigns either resonate or fall flat. For example, a Diwali campaign that uses muted, minimalist visuals because that’s the global brand aesthetic will feel emotionally off in India, where the holiday is visually rich, colorful, and celebratory. Conversely, a Singles Day campaign in China that leans too hard into Western-style romantic imagery will miss the point of the holiday’s self-gifting, tongue-in-cheek spirit. You need a content system that allows for local art direction within global guardrails: flexible color palettes, modular layouts, and copy frameworks that can stretch from playful to reverent depending on the holiday.

Timezones add another layer of complexity that most teams underestimate. When you’re coordinating launches across North America, Europe, and APAC, “midnight launch” means three different days. I’ve seen global promos go live in Europe while the US still has a full business day left on the previous offer, creating pricing conflicts and customer-service headaches. The fix is operational, not creative: define a

(often UTC), standardize your go-live and sunset times in that timezone, and have tooling that lets local teams preview exactly when their assets flip. For major events like Singles Day or Black Friday, create a 24–48 hour “quiet zone” where no other promos can start or end to avoid overlap.
A practical framework that works in the real world is a
(e.g., annual product launches, global brand campaigns), (2)
(e.g., Black Friday/Cyber Monday, end-of-year gifting, Back to School), and (3)
(e.g., Singles Day, Diwali, Golden Week, Ramadan, local school starts). Start by mapping all three layers in a single 12–18 month view. Then, for each market, tag which beats are
. Primary beats get full-funnel campaigns and bespoke assets; secondary beats get adapted global assets; ignored beats are explicitly documented so nobody wastes time localizing irrelevant campaigns.
Once you have that calendar, build a

around it. For each primary beat in each market, work backward: content brief locked 16–20 weeks out, creative concepts 12 weeks out, production 8–10 weeks out, localization and QA 4–6 weeks out, trafficking and QA 1–2 weeks out. Use a shared asset taxonomy so a Japanese marketer can search “Back to School / APAC / Spring” and find relevant modules, not just US fall imagery. And give local teams veto power: if Black Friday is tagged as secondary in their market, they can downsize or skip it in favor of a stronger local event. The goal is not a perfectly synchronized global calendar; it’s a system where global consistency and local relevance can coexist without last-minute heroics.
If you treat seasonal marketing as a translation problem, you’ll always be late, off-tone, or both. Treat it as a

, each with its own timing, emotional register, and commercial profile, and build your operations around that reality. The brands that win aren’t the ones with the flashiest global TV spot; they’re the ones whose April “Back to School” in Japan feels as natural as their August push in the US, whose Diwali work feels made-for-India, and whose Black Friday presence is calibrated to local appetite instead of global habit. That’s the difference between being visible everywhere and being relevant somewhere—which, in seasonal marketing, is where the real money is made.

title: Building Translation Evaluation Frameworks That Actually Prove Quality
excerpt: How to move beyond "does it sound right?" and build measurable, repeatable evaluation frameworks for translation quality using deterministic checks, LLM-as-judge scoring, and automated pipelines.

children.text: Most teams still evaluate translations the way we evaluated copy in the 90s: someone reads it, squints, and says, “Yeah, that sounds right.” That’s fine for a single tagline, but it collapses the moment you’re shipping weekly releases in 20+ locales, mixing human and machine translation, and trying to prove to leadership that your glossary and style guide actually matter. To operate at that scale, you need an evaluation framework that is measurable, repeatable, and explainable—one that turns “sounds right” into numbers you can track, debug, and improve.

The foundation is deterministic checks: things you can verify with zero subjectivity. Start with glossary term presence and forbidden term absence. For example, if your product name must always appear as “Acme Cloud” (never localized, never shortened), you can automatically scan target strings for exact and fuzzy matches. Similarly, if legal has banned “unlimited” in German marketing copy, you can flag any occurrence of
or synonyms. Add formality markers: in languages like German, Spanish, or Japanese, you can programmatically check for pronoun and verb forms (e.g.,
, polite vs. plain Japanese). These checks don’t tell you if the translation is
, but they do tell you if it’s compliant—and compliance is the first line of defense.

You can go surprisingly far with rule-based checks before you touch AI. Regex and simple tokenization can catch punctuation mismatches (missing question marks, stray ellipses), number and unit inconsistencies ("10 GB" in source, "100 GB" in target), and structural issues (placeholders like

missing or moved). For UI strings, you can enforce constraints like “no line breaks” or “max 30 characters” and verify that HTML tags or Markdown syntax are preserved. In one SaaS localization program I worked with, these deterministic checks alone reduced production bugs by ~40% because they caught issues
a human ever saw the string.

Once the basics are guarded by deterministic checks, you can layer in LLM-as-judge scoring to evaluate the harder dimensions: fluency, accuracy, and style adherence. The trick is to treat the LLM like a calibrated evaluator, not a magical oracle. You give it a structured rubric and ask it to score each dimension on a fixed scale—say 1–5—plus a short rationale. For example:
: Is the target text natural and grammatically correct for a native speaker of the target language?
: Does the translation preserve the meaning, tone, and intent of the source, without omissions or additions?
: Does it follow the brand voice and style guide (formality, terminology, sentence length, etc.)?

You can prompt the LLM with your style guide excerpts and glossary, then ask it to score each dimension and highlight specific issues (e.g., “uses informal pronoun where style guide requires formal”).

Baseline comparisons are where this becomes a business tool instead of a science project. To quantify the value of glossaries and style guides, you run controlled experiments: translate the same batch of source strings twice—once with no structured context (no glossary, no style guide, minimal instructions) and once with full context (glossary, style guide, product screenshots, audience description). Then you evaluate both sets with the

deterministic checks and LLM-as-judge rubric. When you can say, “Providing structured context improved accuracy scores from 3.1 to 4.4 and reduced glossary violations by 72%,” you’re no longer arguing about opinions—you’re showing ROI on the time spent maintaining those assets.
Setting up an automated eval pipeline is mostly plumbing, but the details matter. A typical flow looks like this:
: Curate representative strings by content type (UI, marketing, help center, legal). Include edge cases: idioms, technical jargon, short labels, long paragraphs.
: From your MT engine, LLM, or vendor. Store metadata: engine version, prompt, context used.
: A script (Python, Node, whatever your stack is) that outputs pass/fail and counts per check (glossary hits, forbidden terms, placeholder integrity, etc.).
: Batch prompts to your evaluation model with source, target, and context (glossary, style guide snippets). Capture structured JSON: scores per dimension, rationale, error tags.
: Push results into a warehouse or even a spreadsheet, then build simple dashboards: scores over time, by language, by content type, by engine.
Automate this on a schedule (e.g., nightly) or on every model/config change so you can see regressions immediately.

Choosing the right scoring rubric is where senior judgment pays off. Overly granular rubrics (10+ dimensions) look sophisticated but produce noisy, inconsistent scores. I’ve had better results with 3–5 core dimensions: fluency, accuracy, style adherence, terminology compliance, and sometimes “task fit” (e.g., does this translation drive the desired action?). Use a 1–5 or 1–7 scale with clear anchors: what does a 3 vs. a 5 look like in practice? Seed your rubric with real examples from your content. For instance, show the LLM (and human reviewers) a “5” example that nails your brand voice and a “2” example that’s technically correct but stiff and off-tone. Consistency beats complexity; you want scores that move meaningfully when you change engines, prompts, or context—not when the wind changes.
Interpreting results is where teams often stumble. A single score—“average quality 4.2”—is almost useless. Look for

. Are accuracy scores strong but style adherence weak? That suggests your MT engine is fine, but your style guide is either unclear or not being surfaced effectively in prompts. Are deterministic checks clean, but fluency scores low in one language? Maybe your engine is underperforming in that locale, or your training data is skewed. Compare with and without context: if adding the glossary barely moves terminology scores, your glossary might be incomplete or poorly structured. Treat the eval framework like observability for localization: it should help you debug where and why things break, not just tell you that something is “bad.”

The hardest challenge is evaluating across languages when you don’t have fluent reviewers in every locale. This is where LLM-as-judge and cross-lingual checks are especially valuable—but you have to design them carefully. One pattern that works well is
: have an LLM read the source and target, then paraphrase the target back into the source language and highlight any meaning shifts. Another is

: ask the LLM to list specific issues (e.g., “omitted clause about billing,” “mistranslated ‘account’ as ‘invoice’”) in your team’s working language, even if the target is, say, Thai or Polish. You can then spot systemic issues without speaking the language. For critical content (legal, payments), you still need native experts, but the AI layer lets you reserve them for high-risk areas instead of everything.
Finally, don’t forget the human loop. Use your automated framework to

where humans spend time. For example, auto-approve strings that pass all deterministic checks and score ≥4 on all LLM dimensions, and route anything below that threshold to human review. Capture human corrections and feed them back into your eval set: re-run the LLM-as-judge on the corrected translations and compare scores. Over time, your evaluation framework becomes a living asset: a curated, versioned benchmark that reflects your brand, your products, and your markets. That’s when you move from “we hope our translations are good” to “we can prove, with data, that they’re getting better—and we know exactly why.”

title: Structured Content as the Engine of Multi‑Channel Delivery
excerpt: Separating content from presentation is no longer a nice-to-have; it’s the only sustainable way to deliver consistent messaging across web, mobile, email, print, and emerging channels. This article explains why structured content is the prerequisite for multi-channel delivery, how rich text formats like Portable Text enable it, and what organizational shifts are required to move from page-based thinking to reusable content objects.

children.text: For years, most organizations have treated content and presentation as a single, inseparable thing: a “page.” Marketing briefs become landing pages, product updates become blog posts, and everything is laid out directly in a WYSIWYG editor. That approach worked when the website was the primary channel. It completely breaks down when the same message needs to show up consistently on web, mobile apps, email, print, social, and whatever new surface your product team dreams up next quarter. The only way to scale that reality is to separate
it’s displayed (presentation). That’s what structured content gives you.

Structured content means modeling your information as discrete, typed objects with fields and relationships, instead of blobs of formatted HTML tied to a single layout. A product announcement stops being “a blog page” and becomes a content object with fields like

. Presentation rules—fonts, colors, card layouts, email templates—live elsewhere. When you do this well, your web page, mobile app, email template, and in-app notification all pull from the same source of truth, but render it differently based on the needs of each channel.
Rich text is usually where teams get stuck, because they’re used to thinking of it as a single HTML blob. Formats like

solve this by storing rich text as structured data instead of pre-rendered markup. Instead of one long string of HTML, you get an array of blocks and spans, each with types and annotations. A simple paragraph might be a

From that one document, your website can render a full blog post with hero image, long-form body, and a detailed benefits section. Your design system knows how to turn Portable Text blocks into your site’s typography and components. No one is copying and pasting text into a separate “blog page” template; the page is just one view of the same underlying content object.
Now look at the same announcement on social and in email. For social, you might define a
. The renderer for X, LinkedIn, or Facebook turns that into a concise card with character limits and platform-specific image crops. For email, you might use

: a short intro, a bulleted list, and a button. The email template doesn’t need to know anything about the blog layout; it just knows how to map those fields into a responsive email design. If the product name changes, you update it once in the source document, and every channel—web, social, email—reflects the change on the next publish.

from the same structured content: maybe it’s a specific
field on the announcement, or a smart truncation of the
with channel-specific rules. The in-app renderer knows that
